title: Runtime Lifecycle
use_for: defining runtime phase boundaries after startup
structuring update, suspend, and shutdown order
making host lifecycle explicit and reviewable
assigning system ownership to lifecycle phases
keeping runtime orchestration deterministic after bootstrap
do_not_use_for: raw performance tuning without lifecycle ownership concerns
rules: make runtime phases explicit and finite
separate startup orchestration from steady-state lifecycle control
define who can run in each phase and why
make shutdown order as explicit as startup order
prefer one authoritative host lifecycle per runtime surface
procedure: Name the runtime host and its lifecycle phases.
Define enter, update, suspend, resume, and shutdown boundaries as needed.
Assign system ownership to each phase explicitly.
Define transition rules and failure behavior per phase.
Verify that shutdown and recovery paths are reviewable.

primary_when: the request is specifically about runtime phase design, update ordering, host lifecycle, or shutdown sequencing
the request is about explicit lifecycle ownership after startup
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about UI composition, metadata parsing, or one isolated feature's local behavior

solves: how to keep runtime orchestration explicit after bootstrap has finished
how to make update and shutdown order deterministic and reviewable
how to avoid hidden lifecycle behavior spread across unrelated systems

core_principles: Startup is not the whole lifecycle; steady-state and shutdown need equal clarity.
A runtime host should expose explicit phase ownership rather than hidden callbacks.
Lifecycle transitions should explain when systems may run, pause, recover, or stop.
constraints: Lifecycle control must stay inspectable without tracing hidden registration side effects.
Shutdown and failure paths must not rely on optional systems being healthy.
Runtime phases should be stable enough that profiling and debugging can map behavior to them.
decision_rules: Use runtime-lifecycle as the owner when the problem is no longer startup wiring but the host's steady-state or shutdown orchestration.
Keep bootstrap thin by moving recurring runtime coordination into explicit lifecycle phases.
If a system's allowed execution window is unclear, define a lifecycle contract before tuning implementation details.
Attach diagnostics when lifecycle transitions, failures, or shutdown recovery need visibility.

preferred_patterns: named runtime phases with explicit allowed work
phase contracts that separate startup, steady-state, and teardown concerns
avoid_patterns: shutdown order inferred from disposal side effects
multiple overlapping lifecycle owners for one runtime surface
phase transitions triggered implicitly through service resolution

recipes.name: Define a deterministic host lifecycle
recipes.when: Use when a runtime host must orchestrate startup, steady-state execution, and shutdown without hidden behavior.
steps: List the lifecycle phases the host actually needs.
Assign each system to the phases where it may run.
Define explicit transition points and failure behavior.
Verify shutdown order releases dependent systems in reverse-safe order.

pitfalls: Treating bootstrap as if it solves steady-state orchestration automatically.
Letting systems acquire lifecycle behavior through hidden callbacks or registration hooks.
Ignoring shutdown order until failures or teardown bugs appear.
review_checklist: Are runtime phases named and explicit?
Is recurring orchestration kept out of bootstrap?
Can shutdown order be read directly from the design?
Are lifecycle transitions visible enough for profiling and debugging?

references.title: Local Reference: Runtime Lifecycle
references.interpretation: Use this first for host phase design, steady-state orchestration, and deterministic shutdown policy.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for explicit runtime lifecycle design.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework Bootstrap
references.interpretation: Use this to keep lifecycle design aligned with startup and composition-root boundaries.
references.why: Use to separate bootstrap responsibilities from longer-lived lifecycle ownership.
